docs.title: Python Data Structures Cheat Sheet
docs.text: Type Syntax Ordered Mutable Unique Only Search/Fetch Complexity List [...] ✅ ✅ ❌ By Index O(1) By Value O(n) Tuple (...) ✅ ❌ ❌ By Index O(1) Set {...} ❌ ✅ ✅ By Value O(1) Dict {k: v} ✅* ✅ Keys: ✅ By Key O(1)

docs.title: Core Concepts
docs.text:
• The "Hash" Advantage: Sets and Dictionaries use a Hash Table. This is why searching for a value in a Set with 1 million items is just as fast as searching in a Set with 10 items (O(1)).
  • The List Penalty: Searching for a value in a List requires Python to look at every single item one by one (O(n)). As your list grows, your code slows down.
  • Immutability: A Tuple is the only one in this list that cannot be changed after creation. This makes it "Hashable" and safe to use as a Dictionary key.
  • Dict Ordering: Since Python 3.7, Dictionaries remember the order in which you added items, but they are still optimized for key-based lookups, not index-based lookups.

docs.title: Example of Set of Tuples
docs.text:
# A set of (x, y) coordinates
visited_spots = {(0, 0), (1, 5), (10, 20)}

# Even if this set had 10 million tuples, 
# this check is O(1)
if (10, 20) in visited_spots:
    print("Found it!")

docs.title: 🧩 Functional Programming in Python
docs.text: Functional programming (FP) is a paradigm that treats computation as the evaluation of mathematical functions and avoids changing-state or mutable data.

docs.title: 🛠 The "Big Three"
docs.text: These functions allow you to process data without using explicit for loops.

docs.title: 1. Map
docs.text:
Applies a function to every item in an iterable.

numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]

# Using lambda to square each number
squared = list(map(lambda x: x**2, numbers))
# Output: [1, 4, 9, 16, 25]

docs.title: 2. Filter
docs.text:
Filters items based on a condition (must return True or False).

numbers = range(10)

# Keep only even numbers
evens = list(filter(lambda x: x % 2 == 0, numbers))
# Output: [0, 2, 4, 6, 8]

docs.title: 3. Reduce
docs.text:
Performs a rolling computation on sequential pairs of values to return a single value.

Import Required

In Python 3, reduce was moved to the functools module.

from functools import reduce

# Factorial Example: 5! = (5 * 4 * 3 * 2 * 1)
n = 5
factorial = reduce(lambda x, y: x * y, range(1, n + 1))
# Output: 120

docs.title: 🧪 Pure Functions
docs.text:
A function is pure if it:

  1. Returns the same output for the same input every time.
  2. Has no side effects (it doesn't change global variables or modify the input list).
# Pure: Result depends only on inputs
def multiply(a, b):
    return a * b

# Impure: Modifies an external variable (side effect)
total = 0
def add_to_total(n):
    global total
    total += n

docs.title: 🍛 Currying
docs.text:
Currying is the practice of breaking down a function that takes multiple arguments into a series of functions that each take a single argument.

def multiply(a):
    def with_b(b):
        return a * b
    return with_b

# Usage
double = multiply(2)
print(double(10)) # Output: 20

docs.title: 🎨 Decorators
docs.text:
Decorators allow you to "wrap" another function to extend its behavior without permanently modifying it. This is a primary pattern in Python web frameworks and AWS Lambda logic.

def debug_log(func):
    def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
        print(f"Executing {func.__name__}...")
        result = func(*args, **kwargs)
        print("Finished execution.")
        return result
    return wrapper

@debug_log
def say_hi(name):
    return f"Hi {name}"

# Output:
# Executing say_hi...
# Finished execution.
# 'Hi [name]'

docs.title: 🚀 List Comprehensions vs. Map/Filter
docs.text: In Python, "Pythonic" code usually favors readability and speed. Here is how to choose the right tool for the job.

docs.title: 1. Use List Comprehensions When...
docs.text:
• The logic is simple: [x**2 for x in nums] is unbeatable for clarity.
  • You need readability: It reads like a natural sentence from left to right.
  • You need speed: For simple operations, the internal C-optimization of comprehensions beats the overhead of a Python lambda call.

docs.title: 2. Use map() or filter() When...
docs.text:
• You have a named function: ✅ Good: map(int, list_of_strings)
  • ❌ Redundant: [int(x) for x in list_of_strings]
  • You need Lazy Evaluation: If you have a 10GB dataset, map creates an iterator. It processes items one-by-one only as you request them. A list comprehension [] would attempt to load all 10GB into RAM immediately and crash the program.

docs.title: 📊 Comparison Summary
docs.text: Tool Result Type Evaluation Memory Usage Best For... List Comp [] List Eager (Now) High Small/Medium data, speed, readability. Generator () Iterator Lazy (Later) Minimal Massive datasets, infinite streams. Map / Filter Iterator Lazy (Later) Minimal Using existing named functions (e.g., int, len).

docs.title: 💡 The "Pro" Interview Answer
docs.text: If an interviewer asks how to handle a 100GB file without crashing, you describe Lazy Evaluation. You explain that while a List Comprehension is faster for small tasks, a Generator Expression or map() is required for memory safety because they yield one item at a time.

docs.title: What Is a Data Structure?
docs.text: Data structures are just organizational tools that allow for more advanced algorithms.

docs.title: What Is an Algorithm?
docs.text:
An algorithm is a finite sequence of well-defined, computer-implementable instructions. In short, an algorithm is:

  • Defined: there is a specific sequence of steps that performs a task
  • Unambiguous: there is a "correct" and "incorrect" interpretation of the steps
  • Implementable: it can be executed using software and hardware

docs.title: Exponents
docs.text:
An exponent indicates how many times a number is to be multiplied by itself. For example:

\(5^3\) = 5 * 5 * 5 = 125

square = 2 ** 2
# square = 4

cube = 2 ** 3
# cube = 8

docs.title: Logarithms
docs.text: A logarithm is the inverse of an exponent. While exponents grow numbers very quickly, logarithms shrink them, making them essential for calculating Time Complexity.

docs.title: The Core Concept
docs.text:
If \(2^4 = 16\), then the logarithm is the answer to the question: "To what power must we raise 2 to get 16?"

  • Exponential: \(2^4 = 16\)
  • Logarithmic: \(\log_2(16) = 4\)

"\(\log_2(16)\)" is read as "log base 2 of 16". It means "the number of times 2 must be multiplied by itself to equal 16."

docs.title: Why This Matters for Big O
docs.text:
In Big O notation, we usually assume base 2. When you see O(log n), it means the algorithm reduces the problem size by half in every step (e.g., Binary Search).

  • If \(n = 1,000,000\), a linear \(O(n)\) search takes 1,000,000 steps.
  • A logarithmic \(O(\log n)\) search takes only ~20 steps.
